_meta.title: AI Transparency Index
_meta.lede: How openly the major AI providers let the public see what their AI costs — in dollars, in data, and in water and energy. We grade transparency, not the underlying footprint: whether a real figure is findable at all, and who had to surface it. 'Not disclosed' is a finding, not a gap to paper over.

axes.label: Environmental impact
axes.note: Capacity-weighted water & energy disclosure across known AI data center sites. Scored below.

axes.label: Pricing
axes.note: Criteria not yet defined — Not yet scored.

axes.label: Data practices
axes.note: Criteria not yet defined — Not yet scored.

_meta.methodology: We grade three independent axes — pricing, data practices, and environmental impact — separately, and never average them into one score. Four-state system: 🟢 Transparent (the company discloses it itself) · 🟡 Partial (a real figure is public, but only via a regulator, utility, or watchdog — not the company) · 🔴 Opaque (nothing public) · ⚪ Not yet assessed (we haven't checked yet — which is not the same as opaque). For environmental impact specifically: we track each provider's known AI data center sites and grade every site on water/energy disclosure, then roll them up into a capacity-weighted average — bigger sites carry more weight. That means a large opaque site can't hide behind a small transparent one, and one bad site doesn't sink an otherwise-open provider either. Every grade links to its sources. Pricing and data-practices criteria aren't defined yet, so those axes are marked 'Not yet scored.'

grade_legend.label: Partial — public only via regulator, utility or watchdog

caveats: Site coverage is currently small — 5 sites across 4 providers — and expanding over time. That reflects our research pace, not a judgment on any provider we haven't listed yet.
Most 🟡 grades today exist only because of local advocacy groups and utility-records work — not because any law requires the company to disclose. That gap is itself part of the story.
Grades are re-verified periodically, not continuously — a site can move between checks.
This is Legerly's independent read of public information, not a regulatory finding. Tell us if we got something wrong.

detail.title: Disclosure quality by dimension
detail.note: A closer look at how complete each company's own environmental reporting is, across six disclosure dimensions. This lens grades a company's self-reporting rather than the 'can the public find out at all' question the site scores above answer — so here 🟢 means good disclosure, 🟡 partial, 🔴 none or poor. We grade what each company DISCLOSES about the AI compute it controls, not the footprint itself. Rented-vs-owned rule: each company is graded only on what IT publishes, regardless of who owns the building — so a renter that stays silent (OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI) has disclosed nothing, while one that publishes facility detail gets credit. Cross-board: no company separates AI-specific from general data-center footprint, none discloses indirect (power-generation) water — LBNL estimates indirect ≈ 12× direct — and there is no U.S. federal water-disclosure mandate as of mid-2026.
caveats: We grade DISCLOSURE quality, not true environmental performance. A low grade means opacity, not necessarily high impact — a genuine closed-loop site (e.g. Abilene) may use little water yet still score 🔴 for not publishing numbers.

Withdrawal ≠ consumption, and direct ≠ indirect. No company discloses indirect (power-generation) water, which LBNL estimates at ~12× direct — so every 'water positive' claim understates the true system footprint.

Some gradings are genuinely ambiguous and are flagged, not silently resolved: Meta's site-level cell sits between 🟡/🔴; Microsoft's 'water positive' claim is aggressive relative to its thin data-center-specific disclosure.
Conflicting figures are shown as ranges, not picks — e.g. Google Council Bluffs 2024 ≈ 1.0B gal consumed / 1.3B potable / 1.4B withdrawn depending on source and metric.

The xAI/OpenAI/Anthropic non-disclosure finding rests partly on independent trackers (Stanford Foundation Model Transparency Index, Dec 2025; Earth911, 2026), but the underlying fact — no company environmental report exists — is directly verifiable from the absence of any such document.

site_level.note: Reports water for 24 individual data centers across the US/Canada (e.g. Council Bluffs, Pflugerville) — the only major operator to do so.
site_level.source: Google 2024 Environmental Report; MOST Policy Initiative

ai_split.note: Published median per-Gemini-prompt figures (0.24 Wh, 0.26 mL water, 0.03 gCO₂e) but facility reports don't split AI training from general load.

comparability.note: No fleet WUE reported; per-prompt figures use market-based carbon and exclude indirect water — UC-Riverside's Shaolei Ren said Google is 'just hiding the critical information.'

replenishment.note: Reports replenishment by watershed and runs watershed-health assessments, but returned water often sits in different watersheds than those drained.

energy_source.note: Reports 64% carbon-free energy by grid region, but market-based; total emissions up ~51% since 2019.

verification.note: Figures largely self-reported with limited third-party assurance on selected metrics.

site_level.note: Only a single global total (2.5B gal withdrawn, 2025); no site-level and no historical tables, making trend analysis impossible.

ai_split.note: Lumps all data centers together; no AI-workload breakout.

comparability.note: Claims WUE 0.12 L/kWh is 'over 7× better' than the 0.84 industry avg, but the per-kWh chart compares its whole fleet against Google's power-intensive AI-specific facilities.

replenishment.note: 'Returned two-thirds' via infrastructure/offset projects in different communities; a leaked internal plan showed intent to keep data-center water use secret.

energy_source.note: Renewable-matching claims with little disclosed grid-mix detail for AI facilities; PUE 1.15 (2024).

verification.note: Independent assurance over selected sustainability metrics.

site_level.note: Reports company-wide water only; no data-center or site-level consumption, though gives some regional recycled-water percentages (Quincy 74%, Singapore 99%).

ai_split.note: No AI-vs-total split; global operational aggregate.

comparability.note: Reports WUE consistently (0.27 L/kWh, 2025; down from 2.3 historically) but declares 'water positive' without publishing full data-center consumption.

replenishment.note: Claims fleet-wide 'water positive' in FY25 via replenishment offsets, many in different watersheds than those consumed.

energy_source.note: Renewable matching and hydrogen-backup pilots disclosed, but actual grid mix at AI sites opaque.

verification.note: Assurance over selected metrics; WUE methodology published.

site_level.note: Discloses company-wide plus a data-center water aggregate and watershed-level restoration, but not per-site consumption.

comparability.note: Reports PUE (1.08) and restoration volumes consistently but is selective on absolute data-center water.

replenishment.note: Restoration targeted to high/medium water-stress regions and reported by watershed (1.59B gal returned in 2024).

energy_source.note: 11,700+ MW renewables contracted, but the Louisiana Hyperion campus relies on ~10 new gas plants.

verification.note: Water-restoration volumes verified by LimnoTech; selected assurance.

site_level.note: No corporate environmental disclosure; all figures (water ~3–5M gal/day) come from utility MLGW and third parties, not xAI.

energy_source.note: Ran unpermitted on-site methane gas turbines; EPA ruled permits required (Jan 15 2026); no self-disclosed energy mix.

verification.note: No third-party-verified environmental data; no CDP.

site_level.note: No numeric site data; only qualitative Abilene 'closed-loop / initial fill ≈ two Olympic pools' language.

replenishment.note: No replenishment claim; Abilene closed-loop framed qualitatively.

energy_source.note: Abilene runs on gas + grid; OpenAI publishes no energy-mix or Scope data.

verification.note: No sustainability report, no CDP, no assurance.

site_level.note: No environmental report; the Transparency Hub covers AI safety only.

ai_split.note: Nothing disclosed; FMTI notes water use is called 'proprietary.'

energy_source.note: No clean-power PPA to date; data-center carbon intensity among the highest of peers; only an electricity-cost commitment (Feb 11 2026).
